items.evidence_text:
Client: Thanks. I’m self-employed and income’s a bit uneven, and with my kid being so young I just want a plan that doesn’t blow up if the market drops.
Advisor: Got it. And I’ll keep this pretty structured and efficient. Let’s start with income at the household level—what’s your gross income for the year, all-in?
Client: All-in, gross for the year is 111450.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And that’s coming from your business—do you think of it as a base plus something variable, or mostly one stream?
Client: It’s basically two streams. I track it like salary and bonus, but both hit monthly.

Client: Bonus is 76250 annually.
Advisor: Perfect. Next, spending—about what are your total monthly expenses these days, just to run the household?
Client: Roughly fifty-five hundred a month—5500.
Advisor: Okay. And within that, do you have any monthly debt payments—credit cards, auto loan, student loans, anything like that?
Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.

Client: Thanks. I’m self-employed and income’s a bit uneven, and with my kid being so young I just want a plan that doesn’t blow up if the market drops.
Advisor: Got it. And I’ll keep this pretty structured and efficient. Let’s start with income at the household level—what’s your gross income for the year, all-in?
Client: All-in, gross for the year is 111450.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And that’s coming from your business—do you think of it as a base plus something variable, or mostly one stream?
Client: It’s basically two streams. I track it like salary and bonus, but both hit monthly.

Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.
Advisor: Great—clean slate on debt. Let’s shift to what you’ve built up. About how much do you have in investable assets total—so accounts you can invest from, not counting the home value?
Client: Total investable assets are 349900.
Advisor: Thanks. I’m going to break that down by account so we don’t miss anything. First, your taxable brokerage—what’s the current value there?
Client: The taxable brokerage is 216400.

Client: A small private markets position—5900—on a retirement platform.
Advisor: Thanks. And then separately from investable assets, you mentioned you own your home—what’s the current value you use for your primary residence?
Client: I use 203900 for the primary residence value.
Advisor: Great. Just to confirm on liabilities: no mortgage balance, no other loans outstanding, and no credit card balance you’re carrying month to month?
Client: Correct—no loans outstanding and no carried balances.

Client: Correct, none right now.
Advisor: Alright, Robert—assets and debts are nice and clean in my notes. Let’s zoom out to how you like to invest. When markets get choppy, how would you describe your risk tolerance?
Client: Conservative. I’m pretty conservative—my first question is usually, “what’s the worst-case?”
Advisor: Yep, that tracks with what you’ve been saying. When you say conservative, is that more like “I can’t stomach big swings,” or more “I just don’t want to take unnecessary risk”?
Client: More the second—don’t take unnecessary risk. I can handle some movement, but I’m conservative overall.

Client: More the second—don’t take unnecessary risk. I can handle some movement, but I’m conservative overall.
Advisor: Got it. Next, just for planning assumptions—what state are you filing from right now, Robert?
Client: NJ.
Advisor: New Jersey—okay. And country-wise, you’re in the US, right?
Client: Yes, US.

Advisor: Got it. And I’ll keep this pretty structured and efficient. Let’s start with income at the household level—what’s your gross income for the year, all-in?
Client: All-in, gross for the year is 111450.
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And that’s coming from your business—do you think of it as a base plus something variable, or mostly one stream?
Client: It’s basically two streams. I track it like salary and bonus, but both hit monthly.
Advisor: Helpful. What’s the annual amount you’re calling “salary” in that split?

Advisor: Hi Robert—good to meet you. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client: Thanks. I’m self-employed and income’s a bit uneven, and with my kid being so young I just want a plan that doesn’t blow up if the market drops.
Advisor: Got it. And I’ll keep this pretty structured and efficient. Let’s start with income at the household level—what’s your gross income for the year, all-in?

Advisor: In the household, is it just you—how many adults are we planning for?
Client: Just me—1 adult.
Advisor: Got it. And marital status—are you currently single, married, divorced, anything like that?
Client: I’m single.
Advisor: Okay. And dependents—anyone you financially support, like kids?

Client: Yes—1 dependent. My kid.
Advisor: Thanks. And last one in this bucket: for tax planning, what bracket do you generally fall into right now?
Client: 37%.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. That helps with how aggressive we are about tax drag in the taxable account.
Advisor: While we’re on the “big picture,” you mentioned you’re self-employed—your income we captured as salary and bonus paid monthly, totaling 111450 annually. Still the right framing?
